blocks.content:
The query defines what data Grafana monitors. You point the rule at a data source, choose a metric, filter it down to what matters, and apply operations that transform the raw data into something useful.

This milestone uses the **grafanacloud-play-prom** Prometheus data source from the QuickPizza tutorial data sources. You build the query in **Builder** mode, targeting a real HTTP request counter from the QuickPizza demo service.

blocks.title: Select the QuickPizza Prometheus data source

blocks.content: You'll point the alert rule at the Prometheus data source that came with the QuickPizza tutorial data sources.
In the **Define query and alert condition** section, open the data source picker and select **grafanacloud-play-prom**.
blocks.tooltip: Pick the Prometheus data source from the QuickPizza tutorial data sources.

blocks.content: The Prometheus query editor has two modes: **Builder** (visual, point-and-click) and **Code** (write PromQL by hand). Builder is the fastest way in when you're new to Prometheus, and it's the default for new queries.
Above the query field, confirm **Builder** is selected. If you're on **Code**, click **Builder** to switch.
The **Metric** field is where you tell Prometheus which time series to query. QuickPizza exposes an HTTP request counter you can alert on.

Click the **Metric** drop-down and select `quickpizza_server_http_request_duration_seconds_count`.

If you can't find the Metric from the dropdown, Click the **Open metric explorer** button and search for the metric.

This is a counter that increments every time QuickPizza handles an HTTP request. It's exactly the kind of counter you'd alert on in production.

blocks.title: Narrow down with a label filter

blocks.content: QuickPizza has multiple services. A label filter narrows the query to just the one you care about — the public API, which handles customer traffic.
Below the metric picker, in the **Label filters** row, open the **Label** drop-down and select `service_name`.

In the value picker next to `service_name`, select `quickpizza-public-api`.

Your query now returns only the HTTP request counter for QuickPizza's public API.

blocks.title: Convert the counter to a rate

blocks.content: Counters only ever go up, so the raw value isn't useful for alerting. Applying **Rate** turns the counter into a per-second rate — how many requests QuickPizza is currently serving. That's what you actually want to compare against a threshold.

Click **+ Operations** to add a function to your query. In the picker, select **Range > Rate** and `$__rate_interval`.

Grafana wraps your query with `rate(...[$__rate_interval])` under the hood — but you never touched PromQL.

Click **Run queries** to preview the data your query returns.

You should see a line that fluctuates in the low single digits — the real-time HTTP request rate for QuickPizza's public API. If the preview is empty, double-check that you selected `grafanacloud-play-prom` and the metric name is correct.
Your query now returns a real per-second request rate from QuickPizza. In the next milestone, you set the condition that decides when this rate is high enough to fire an alert.
